text: The activity and stability of catalysts are of prime interest in many catalytic reactions. The catalytic performance of supported metal nanoparticles is thought to be governed by the formation and dynamics of the active sites during catalytic reactions. Herein, we present a novel light-activated method for controlling the structural evolution and compositional adjustment of Cu species on TiO2 during low-temperature MSR. Our results indicate that under dark conditions, the active Cu species were found to be oxidized into inactive Cu2O by *CH3O species that are strongly adsorbed on the Cu species, leading to a fast thermal deactivation of the catalyst. Interestingly, when UV light was added into the reaction system, the activity of the catalyst was successfully restored by exciting the charge carriers on the TiO2 support that reduced Cu2O into active Cu species. Low intensity solar light (35 mW cm−2) of 1724.1 μmol g−1 min−1 for H2 production, which was much higher than that of the noble metal catalysts such as Ru/C (1241 μmol g−1 min−1) and Pd/C (1472 μmol g−1 min−1) and most thermocatalytic catalysts (Table S6). Operando-XAS and -Raman combined with DFT simulations reveals the unique light-induced reduction of Cu2O into active Cu species through light–matter–reactant interactions at the catalyst surface.
